feature_name: Dual-mode operation: Root Complex (RC) and Endpoint (EP)
description: The PCIe core supports both Root Complex and Endpoint operation selectable via bootstrap pins and software. At power-up PCIESSMODE[1:0] determines the default mode, and software can override using the DEVSTAT register. As an Endpoint, both legacy and native endpoint behaviors are supported. This flexibility allows the same silicon to interoperate in various topologies. Mode selection impacts available features such as legacy INTx reception.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 1.4.1; Chapter 1 Introduction, Functional Block Diagram notes (PCIESSMODE pins, DEVSTAT)
dependencies: Bootstrap pins PCIESSMODE[1:0]; DEVSTAT register; Proper reset sequence
Remarks: Choose mode early during initialization; verify enumeration expectations per selected role.

feature_name: Endpoint modes: Legacy and Native EP
description: The Endpoint function can operate as a legacy endpoint or a native PCIe endpoint. Legacy mode aligns with conventional PCI behaviors for compatibility. Native mode leverages PCIe capabilities fully. Selection follows bootstrap configuration and can be altered by software. Mode selection affects interrupt signaling and configuration handling.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 1.4.1 (Legacy EP and Native EP)
dependencies: PCIESSMODE[1:0]; DEVSTAT register
Remarks: Prefer native EP unless system requires legacy semantics; verify host BIOS/RC expectations.

feature_name: PCIe SerDes PHY with PLL and CDR
description: The integrated SerDes PHY provides the analog layer including PLL, transceivers, CDR, and serializers/deserializers. It handles reference clock conditioning and supports test logic and scrambler. PLL lock detection is provided via status registers for reliable bring-up. Proper configuration of bandwidth and VCO range is required for clocking quality. PHY generates functional clocks for controller domains from the input differential reference.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 1.4.2; Section 2 SerDes Configuration (bits ENDIVCLK, VRANGE, SLEEPPLL); PCIE_SERDES_STS LOCK
dependencies: Stable reference clock; SERDES configuration registers; Power rails/termination
Remarks: Validate reference clock quality and configure PLL bandwidth per system clocking architecture.

feature_name: Lane configuration: x1 or x2 single-link
description: The subsystem exposes a single PCIe link that can be configured for one or two lanes. It cannot be split to connect two independent x1 devices and does not support switch functionality. Lane enablement is controlled in port logic with encoded values. Design ensures a single target connection for simplicity and signal integrity. Link width directly impacts throughput and equalization settings.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 1.5 Supported Use Case; PL_GEN2.LN_EN (Table 3-197)
dependencies: PL_GEN2 register; Board routing for x1/x2; Partner lane width negotiation
Remarks: Ensure board routing and partner support match configured width; monitor negotiated width in DEBUG registers.

feature_name: PCIe Gen2 support with directed speed change and TX controls
description: The controller complies with PCIe Base Specification 2.0 and supports Gen1 and Gen2 operation. Software can initiate a directed speed change after Gen1 link-up to transition to Gen2. TX parameters such as de-emphasis and swing are programmable. Fast training sequence count is configurable to aid link stability. These controls enable tuning for compliance and channel characteristics.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 1.6; PL_GEN2 (Table 3-197: DIR_SPD, DEEMPH, CFG_TX_SWING, NUM_FTS)
dependencies: PL_GEN2 register; Stable link at Gen1 before speed change; Proper channel margin
Remarks: Perform compliance testing per platform; only direct speed change when signal integrity margins are met.

feature_name: VBUSM 128-bit master/slave interfaces to CPU fabric
description: A 128-bit VBUSM master port services inbound transfers into the SoC, while a 128-bit VBUSM slave port supports MMR access and outbound data. This separation enables efficient DMA-style movements and register access paths. The fabric bridges clock and domain boundaries as required. Bandwidth aligns with PCIe negotiated speed and link width. Software orchestrates transfers using SoC DMA engines.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 1.4.3 VBUSM (Configuration and DMA Access Interface)
dependencies: SoC interconnect availability; DMA engine (e.g., EDMA); Clock domain crossings
Remarks: Use EDMA for high-throughput transfers; ensure coherency and ordering settings match system needs.

feature_name: Inbound address translation with 4 programmable regions
description: Four inbound regions map PCIe TLP addresses to internal bus addresses. Each region matches a target BAR and translates using start address high/low and an offset register. Regions can split non-contiguous BAR spaces into multiple internal areas. This enables flexible mapping of host accesses into SoC memory/peripherals. Programming order must consider multiple matching BARs and highest-start-address rule.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2.7.2.1 and 2.7.2 (IB_BARn, IB_STARTn_HI/LO, IB_OFFSETn); Example/Table 2-6
dependencies: IB_BARn/IB_STARTn/IB_OFFSETn registers; BAR configuration; Enumeration complete
Remarks: Avoid overlaps; program regions in ascending start addresses; validate translations with test reads/writes.

feature_name: BAR0 dedicated to application registers (Address Space 0)
description: Inbound transactions targeting BAR0 are always routed to the PCIe application registers. This fixed mapping applies in both RC and EP modes and cannot be disabled. It allows remote control and MSI functionality even without local EP software. Software must not attempt to remap BAR0 via inbound translator. Access control to registers should be handled at system level.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2.7.2.2 BAR0 Exception for In-Bound Address Translation
dependencies: BAR0 configuration; Inbound translator settings
Remarks: Reserve BAR0 size appropriately; restrict external write access to critical control registers via system policy.

feature_name: BAR1 start-address mirroring for inbound translation
description: Inbound region for BAR1 can use the BAR1 value as the translation start when the region start is programmed to zero. This simplifies dynamic mapping by anchoring translation to enumerated BAR1. Only one inbound window is available in this mode and it must be relative to BAR1. Other BARs and regions cannot be used concurrently for inbound translation if this feature is enabled. It is useful for simple memory windows controlled by the host.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2.7.2.3 Using BAR1 Value As Start Address
dependencies: BAR1 programmed; One inbound region reserved; Proper size/mask settings
Remarks: Use when a single host-controlled window suffices; document limitation that other inbound regions are unavailable.

feature_name: MSI interrupt generation and reception (32 vectors grouped)
description: The subsystem supports 32 MSI vectors with internal grouping where every four vectors share an interrupt event to the CPU. MSI can be used in both RC and EP modes with enable, clear, and status registers. Reception sets status bits which must be cleared and acknowledged via IRQ_EOI. MSI write-trigger semantics and race conditions require careful driver sequencing. Either MSI or legacy interrupts should be used, not both simultaneously for an EP.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2.14.4/2.14.6; Table 2-10; MSIn_IRQ_EN_SET/CLR/STATUS; IRQ_EOI; Example MSI6 at offsets 0x160/0x164 (Tables 3-48, 3-49)
dependencies: MSIn_* registers; IRQ controller; Proper BAR0 access for MSI in EP
Remarks: Clear status then EOI to avoid lost events; document vector-to-event-number mapping from Table 2-10.

feature_name: Legacy INTx support (RC mode only)
description: Legacy INTA/INTB/INTC/INTD messages are supported for Root Complex operation. The PCIe specification does not permit EPs to receive legacy interrupts, and the subsystem aligns with that. Events are mapped to interrupt numbers in the PCIESS for the CPU. Use MSI in preference where possible for scalability. Driver must mask/unmask and acknowledge per event mapping.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2.14 and Table 2-10 (INTA–INTD RC-only)
dependencies: Interrupt controller; Configuration space command bits (INTx disable)
Remarks: Prefer MSI for performance; use INTx only when required by legacy devices or firmware constraints.

feature_name: Advanced Error Reporting (AER) and error interrupts
description: If enabled, the subsystem generates interrupts for fatal, non-fatal, and correctable errors in RC mode and supports AXI bridge error reporting in both modes. AER requires enabling error message reporting in the Root Error Command register. The interrupt can be delivered via MSI or internally as INTx in RC. Software must parse capability structures to determine error sources. Proper handling prevents system deadlock and facilitates recovery.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2.14.5.3; Table 2-10 error group; Root Error Command (RC_ERR_CMD)
dependencies: PCIe Capability/AER structures; RC_ERR_CMD; MSI/INTx routing
Remarks: Enable only after drivers are ready; log syndrome registers before reset or recovery actions.

feature_name: End-to-End CRC (ECRC) generation and checking
description: The controller exposes controls to enable ECRC generation on transmitted TLPs and checking on received TLPs. Capability and control bits indicate whether the endpoint/root complex is ECRC-capable. Enabling ECRC improves data integrity across complex fabrics. Software must ensure peers support ECRC to avoid interoperability issues. Use policy-based enablement during enumeration.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2.16.2.3.1; PCIE_ACCR bits: ECRC Generation/Check Enable/Capable
dependencies: PCIE_ACCR register; Peer capability discovery via configuration space
Remarks: Negotiate ECRC support; enable after verifying both ends advertise capability to avoid malformed TLP handling.

feature_name: Active State Power Management (ASPM) L0/L0s/L1; L3 supported; L2 not supported
description: Hardware supports ASPM transitions among L0, L0s, and L1 for power savings. L3 (power-off) is supported by default, while L2 is not supported on this subsystem. Software can also drive link to L1 via device D-states. Transitions are controlled via PCIe configuration space and may be autonomous. Proper state handling preserves link stability and wake-up behavior.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2.15.2 and 2.15 (Figures 2-7, 2-8)
dependencies: Configuration space ASPM controls; System power states; Reference clock availability
Remarks: Coordinate with system PM; test wake/resume paths; avoid enabling L1 if latency budgets are tight.

feature_name: PCIe power domain control via PSC with reset considerations
description: Turning the PCIe power domain off resets the module to its initial state. All inbound and outbound traffic must be quiesced on both ends before power-down to avoid hangs. After power-up, internal configuration must be restored and link training restarted. Transition status must be observed via PSC GOSTAT fields per the PSC guide. There is no extra delay after power is turned on before use beyond standard bring-up.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2 (Reset Considerations) p.2-34
dependencies: Platform PSC; Traffic quiesce on both local and remote; Re-initialization sequence
Remarks: Gate clocks and power only after link idle; implement robust quiesce and retrain sequence handlers.

feature_name: LTSSM enable and forced link state control
description: The LTSSM is enabled via a control bit, initiating link negotiation with the partner. Software can force the link to a specific state for diagnostics or special bring-up sequences using PL_FORCE_LINK. For example, skipping Detect and entering POLL_ACTIVE can aid certain loopback tests. Proper use requires careful timing and knowledge of partner behavior. Status must be polled to confirm link-up and training completion.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Table 3-3 CMD_STATUS.LTSSM_EN; Section 2.11.1.1 and PL_FORCE_LINK usage (p.2-32)
dependencies: CMD_STATUS and PL_FORCE_LINK registers; SERDES ready; Partner presence
Remarks: Use forced states only for debug; in production rely on standard LTSSM progression for interoperability.

feature_name: Debug visibility via DEBUG0/DEBUG1 and LTSSM state encoding
description: Dedicated debug registers expose LTSSM state, link-up indication, TS1/TS2 reception, polarity/lane reversal, and PIPE signals. The LTSSM state is encoded with a documented mapping in Appendix A. These registers are read-only and useful for bring-up and failure analysis. They allow verification of link training phases and idle/compliance conditions. Using them reduces time-to-root-cause during interoperability testing.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 3.9.11 DEBUG0; 3.9.12 DEBUG1 (offsets 0x1728, 0x172C); Appendix A Table A-1
dependencies: Access to PCIe MMRs (BAR0 or local); Clock active; LTSSM enabled
Remarks: Poll DEBUG registers during training; log LTSSM code transitions for support cases.

feature_name: PHY loopback and compliance signaling controls
description: The controller supports PHY-level loopback and compliance pattern requests for diagnostics. PIPE_TXDETECTRX_LB and PIPE_TXCOMPLIANCE fields allow entering loopback and compliance modes. Loopback setup involves SerDes configuration and forcing LTSSM states to POLL_ACTIVE per the guide. Compliance support aids electrical validation. Use only in controlled test scenarios.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2 (Loopback procedure) p.2-32; DEBUG1 fields PIPE_TXDETECTRX_LB and PIPE_TXCOMPLIANCE (Table 3-196)
dependencies: SERDES configuration; PL_FORCE_LINK; Test equipment
Remarks: Disable normal traffic before entering loopback; restore standard settings after tests.

feature_name: Hot reset initiation and handling
description: Software can initiate a downstream hot reset using INIT_RST, with bridge flush status provided to ensure no pending transactions. Endpoints may also receive hot reset requests when link is down, which triggers interrupts and LTSSM disablement. Proper sequencing prevents partial resets and lingering transactions. Recovery requires reinitializing application state and retraining the link. This mechanism supports robust recovery from link failures.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Table 3-7 CMD_STATUS: INIT_RST, FLUSH_N; Section 2.14.4.1 Hot Reset Request Interrupt
dependencies: CMD_STATUS register; IRQ handling; Traffic quiesce
Remarks: Write INIT_RST only when FLUSH_N indicates no pending transactions; handle hot reset interrupts promptly.

feature_name: Link down and transaction error interrupts
description: Hardware signals link-down conditions and transaction timeouts/aborts via dedicated interrupts. Link-down recommends resetting the PCIESS and reinitializing all application states. Transaction errors should be handled based on severity per PCIe specification. These events can be routed via MSI or legacy mechanisms depending on mode. Proactive logging aids system reliability.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2.14.6.1 Link down; 2.14.6.2 Transaction Error Interrupts
dependencies: Interrupt controller; Reset handlers; AER configuration (optional)
Remarks: Treat link-down as a full subsystem reset event; rate-limit error handling to avoid storms.

feature_name: Configuration space (1 KB), BAR masks, and TLP attributes
description: The PCIe configuration space implements 1 KB with fixed register offsets irrespective of access method. BAR Mask registers are provided for both Type 0 and Type 1 spaces. Global attributes allow enabling No Snoop and Relaxed Ordering on all outgoing TLPs. These controls influence ordering and caching behaviors across the fabric. Correct setup is essential for coherency and performance.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2.7.3; Section 2-19 (1 KB config); Registers Sections 3-65/3-79 (BAR Masks); Table 3-6 (NO_SNOOP, RELAXED)
dependencies: Configuration access path; System coherency model; Host/peer support
Remarks: Enable NO_SNOOP/RELAXED only when system memory model permits; verify BAR sizing during enumeration.

feature_name: EDMA-based DMA transfers for PCIe read/write
description: High-throughput data movement is achieved by using the SoC EDMA engine with the PCIe VBUSM interfaces. Example configurations demonstrate chunking by maximum payload size and setting up EDMA PARAMs. This offloads CPU and aligns transfers to PCIe payload boundaries. Proper source/destination indexing enables efficient streaming. Interrupt-driven completion is recommended for scalability.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2.9.3.2 Example 2-5 (Memory Read via EDMA)
dependencies: EDMA module; pcieAddr mapping; Max Payload Size; VBUSM connectivity
Remarks: Set aCnt/bCnt to match payload and buffer size; ensure address translation is configured before DMA.

feature_name: Standards compliance and single-link limitation (no switch)
description: The module complies with PCI Express Base Specification Revision 2.0. It exposes a single physical link and cannot operate as a PCIe switch. Consequently, it cannot connect two x1 devices simultaneously. This constraint simplifies configuration and routing. System designs must plan for a single downstream/upstream connection only.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 1.5 Supported Use Case; Section 1.6 Industry Standards Compliance
dependencies: Board topology; System architecture planning
Remarks: Architect system around one link; use external switch devices if fan-out is required.

feature_name: Clocking control and PLL lock monitoring
description: Multiple clock domains are present, with PHY generating functional clocks from the differential reference. PLL lock time is bounded and indicated via a status bit for safe sequencing. Bandwidth selection and VCO range configuration adapt to reference clock quality and cleaning topologies. Sleep modes are available to reduce power. Correct configuration reduces jitter transfer and improves stability.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 1.4.4; Section 2-5 (SERDES CFG fields ENDIVCLK/VRANGE/SLEEPPLL); Section 2-7 (PCIE_SERDES_STS.LOCK)
dependencies: Stable refclk; SERDES configuration; Power sequencing
Remarks: Sample LOCK before enabling LTSSM; choose PLL bandwidth per presence/absence of cleaner PLL.

feature_name: Polarity and lane reversal detection
description: During training the controller can test for receiver polarity reversal and detect lane reversal. Indicators are exposed in debug registers for analysis. These mechanisms improve robustness against routing inversions. They assist in diagnosing bring-up when links fail to train. Use with LTSSM state to pinpoint negotiation failures.
evidence: SPRUGS6D DEBUG1 bits RCVR_REVRS_POL_EN and RMLH_RCVD_LANE_REV (Table 3-196)
dependencies: DEBUG1 register access; Active training session
Remarks: Correlate with channel routing; ensure proper Tx/Rx pairing on PCB to minimize reliance on reversal handling.

feature_name: Power management events and messaging
description: Power management turn-off, acknowledgment, and event messages generate interrupts. Software must act on these to manage D-states and link states coherently. Reception can occur in both RC and EP contexts with mode-specific semantics. Handling includes coordinating device clocks and resets. Proper sequencing prevents spurious wake/sleep transitions.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Table 2-10 group 13 (Power Management and Reset Event Interrupts); Section 2.14.6.3
dependencies: Interrupt controller; PM policy manager; ASPM settings
Remarks: Debounce and serialize PM events; log transitions for diagnostics.

feature_name: Global TLP attributes: No Snoop and Relaxed Ordering
description: Global control allows tagging all outgoing TLPs with No Snoop and/or Relaxed Ordering attributes. This can improve performance when strict ordering and cache coherency are not required. Incorrect use can violate platform memory semantics. Controls are provided via a configuration register. Default is disabled for safety.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Table 3-6 (NO_SNOOP, RELAXED fields)
dependencies: Ordering model of system; Peer acceptance of attributes
Remarks: Enable selectively for DMA windows that bypass CPU caches; validate with system-level benchmarking.

feature_name: Security features
description: Security-specific mechanisms such as access control, privilege filtering, or encryption are not described in the user guide. Register access over BAR0 provides powerful control and should be protected by system policy. ECRC improves integrity but is not a security feature. Any security posture must be implemented at system level. Treat external access as untrusted by default.
evidence: UNKNOWN
dependencies: System security policy; Host firewall/IOMMU (if present)
Remarks: Use IOMMU/MPU and restrict BAR exposures; avoid mapping sensitive regions to inbound windows.
